design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate arena-style levels using eight-way movement and three-button combat (punch/kick/jump) to defeat successive waves of street gang enemies through combo attacks and environmental positioning
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing limited lives while mastering positioning and combo sequencing against numerically superior enemies in arena combat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Street brawler fighting through rival gangs to rescue girlfriend (Western version) or high school delinquent Kunio defending classmate against organized gangs (Japanese version)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Genre-defining beat-em-up that rejected single-axis movement of predecessors (Kung-Fu Master, Shao-lin's Road) in favor of eight-way arena navigation with three-quarter isometric perspective; emphasized combo-based combat requiring enemy stun-lock sequencing rather than simple hit-and-move patterns; introduced grab-and-stomp mechanics for downed enemies; designed semi-autobiographically by Yoshihisa Kishimoto based on his own high school fights and Bruce Lee's Enter the Dragon philosophy of 'knock-down-drag-out' combat

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives (quantity varies by arcade DIP settings; NES version inherits this structure)
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: DIP settings in arcade version allow operators to adjust lives and difficulty parameters; player must master timing and positioning rather than memorize patterns, with combat requiring precise understanding of combo windows and when enemies are vulnerable to grabs

progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear stage progression through five arena stages; boss encounters at end of each stage gate progression
replayability_hooks: Multiple control scheme mastery (running attacks, back-kicks, flying kicks, sit-on pummeling)
Arcade DIP difficulty adjustments allow skill-gated progression

world_building_techniques: Localization strategy: Japanese version (Kunio protecting classmate from rival gangs) vs. Western version (street brawler rescuing girlfriend from mob boss) provide thematic coherence through character context
Visual style differentiation: Western arcade version inspired by 1979 film The Warriors for gangland aesthetic
Character archetypes (rival gangs, organized crime) establish implicit street-culture setting

boss_design.philosophy: Boss characters require accumulated health depletion before becoming grabbable/vulnerable to collar grab mechanics; bosses (except Sabu) actively resist player grab attempts until sufficiently damaged, enforcing phase-like behavior
notable_bosses: Sabu (exception: grabbable at any health state)
Four gang leaders (one per stage before final mob boss)

faction_indicators: Named rival gangs encountered sequentially (implying territorial conflict)
Organized crime hierarchy (mook thugs → gang leaders → mob boss)
danger_signals: Numerically superior enemy waves in each arena
Progressive gang escalation across five stages
environmental_storytelling: Arena-style level design suggests contained conflict zones (school grounds, streets, gang territories)
Visual character differentiation by gang affiliation through sprites
Isometric perspective creates depth suggesting infiltration through gang-controlled spaces
